text: The surgeon begins the flap creation laterally with a vertical oblique incision, staying outside the retro-rectus space. As the dissection progresses medially, a horizontal incision is made across the peritoneum.

The surgeon navigates laterally in the space of Bacros, staying close to the peritoneum due to the patient's minimal preperitoneal fat. A wide incision is made, gradually deepening into the pocket to avoid bleeding and unintentional damage to nearby structures.

The surgeon identifies the transversalis fascia of the direct inguinal hernia, preparing to reduce it. Small zaps of cautery are applied to divide the transversalis sling and coagulate tiny blood vessels to prevent oozing and bleeding, ensuring clear visibility of the tissue planes.

The surgeon carefully separates the testicular vessels from the peritoneum, revealing a clear areolar plane. Fat is removed from the peritoneum laterally, and the small indirect inguinal hernia is reduced. Attention is then directed to the direct inguinal hernia, with the transverse salus fascia visible and serving as a guide for the dissection.

The surgeon uses robotic precision to dissect along the thin sacs without perforating the peritoneum. Laterally, the surgeon pulls to expose the plane between the peritoneum and the vas deferens, while carefully staying against the peritoneum near the obturator lymph node packet to avoid injury.

The surgeon reduces the direct inguinal hernia and proceeds past the midline, advancing two centimeters deep into the retro pubic space. The operation progresses smoothly, with the surgeon maintaining a broad working plane, moving methodically from one area to another before returning to deepen the dissection.

The surgeon adjusts retraction to work across a wide area, ensuring a smooth transition for the mesh from the retropubic space to the apogryl space. The dissection is extended to the obturator space, leaving the obturator fat intact as a natural cork for the obturator foramen.

The surgeon prioritizes the dissection of the cord to obtain the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, exposing the iliopubic tract and confirming the absence of a cord lipoma. The lateral and general femoral nerves are visible, while the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve is less discernible. The dissection progresses past the midline, reaching two centimeters deep into the retro pubic space.

The surgeon identifies a small femoral gap and prepares to place a large 3D Max mid mesh in the pelvic region. The surgical field is limited due to the patient's petite size, requiring adjustment of the right-hand port outside the body.
Using the X-Eye robotic system, the surgeon aligns the ports along the vertical line of the vas deferens and places a stitch at Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon checks for any peritoneum tucked behind the mesh and observes the robust Cooper's ligament. They ensure the medial lower edge of the flap remains flat when the bladder distends. The mesh is positioned over the pubis and superior pubic rami, with an additional stitch placed to prevent the mesh from being swallowed or ballooning out of the direct defect.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture, approximately six inches long, to secure the mesh in place during the inguinal hernia repair. The mesh appears to be laying well, eliminating the need for an additional superior lateral suture.
The surgeon inserts a laparoscopic suction cannula into the abdominal cavity without attaching any suction device, allowing the intra-abdominal pressure to naturally evacuate CO2 from the pocket.

The surgeon uses a six-inch suture to close the surgical site, leaving a small gap near the end. The needle driver is positioned directly into the gap, while the assistant uses a suction cannula to evacuate CO₂ from the pocket. The mesh is checked to ensure it is properly positioned, especially posterior laterally, before running the suture back with two throws to complete the closure.
